# MATH-CS COMPASS

> **Bridging Pure & Applied Mathematics with Computer Science and Machine Learning**

MATH-CS COMPASS is a free educational platform that addresses the gap where computer science students struggle with mathematical foundations while mathematics students lack awareness of practical applications. The curriculum develops rigorous mathematical foundations and introduces application domains at the moment sufficient tools are in hand — creating a virtuous cycle of **math → application preview → deeper math → richer application.**

## Philosophy

Mathematics is the study of **structure**. This platform offers an accessible yet principled look at the mathematical blueprints behind modern computation. Specific tools and frameworks become outdated quickly — the mathematical foundations are eternal.

The curriculum is organized as **two worlds + a bridge + application viewpoints**:

- **The Core (Section I)** — Linear Algebra to Algebraic Foundations. Inherited by every later section.
- **The Continuous World (Section II)** — Calculus, Analysis, Functional Analysis. Smooth change, limits, completeness.
- **The Discrete World (Section IV)** — Logic, Algorithms, Combinatorics, Discrete Topology. Finite and countable structures.
- **The Bridge (Section III)** — Probability and Statistics. Connects discrete counting to continuous distributions.
- **Application Viewpoints (Section V)** — Machine Learning. Not terminal destinations but vantage points where prior tools illuminate real problems and motivate further mathematical depth.

### Three Application Horizons

These are **not terminal goals** but recurring **viewpoints** — intermediate plateaus where accumulated tools illuminate a class of real-world problems and reveal deeper mathematics worth pursuing.

1. **Geometric Deep Learning (GDL)** — *most actively committed.* Symmetry, invariance, and continuous geometry applied to network architectures. Draws on Lie groups, Riemannian geometry, and fiber bundles.
2. **Categorical Deep Learning (CDL)** — *planned, not yet curriculum-ready.* Compositionality, structural abstraction, and functorial reasoning applied to network operations. Draws on category theory, quivers, and string diagrams.
3. **Quantum Computation** — *hedged horizon.* Unitary evolution, spectral decomposition, and Hilbert space geometry — would emerge naturally from the existing Fourier / Lp / spectral track in Section II if pursued.

## Author & Meta

- **Author**: Yusuke Yokota
- **Education**: B.S. Mathematics & B.S. Computer Science, Western Washington University
- **Version**: 3.0.0 (Last Updated: May 2026)
- **Website**: [https://math-cs-compass.com](https://math-cs-compass.com)
- **GitHub**: [https://github.com/yusukey01](https://github.com/yusukey01)

## Curriculum Outline

The curriculum spans **113 pages across five sections**. Each section's landing page contains the full part list and prerequisite map; the file `curriculum.json` at the site root is the canonical machine-readable index.

### Section I: Linear Algebra to Algebraic Foundations (30 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Linear_algebra/linear_algebra.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Linear_algebra/linear_algebra.html)
**Tagline**: From Vector Spaces to Universal Algebraic Structures
Linear systems, vector spaces, eigenvalues, orthogonality, least squares, SVD, Kronecker / tensor products, stochastic matrices, graph Laplacians; the Abstract Algebra block (groups, rings, fields, polynomial rings, integral domains); extension fields and finite fields (foundation for cryptography); the Lie group / Lie algebra series (matrix Lie groups, the matrix exponential, Lie algebras, the Lie correspondence) — opening the symmetry path toward Geometric Deep Learning.

### Section II: Calculus to Optimization & Analysis (31 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Calculus/calculus.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Calculus/calculus.html)
**Tagline**: The Mathematics of Change and Convergence
Derivatives (Jacobians, matrix calculus); optimization (gradient descent, Newton's method, constrained optimization, duality); integration (Riemann, measure theory, Lebesgue); Fourier analysis; metric spaces (convergence, continuity, completeness, connectedness, compactness, equivalence); topological spaces; the Functional Analysis block (Banach and Hilbert spaces, bounded linear operators, dual spaces and Riesz representation, weak topologies and Banach-Alaoglu, spectral theory of compact operators, RKHS); Lp space theory (construction, Hölder / Minkowski inequalities, completeness via Riesz-Fischer, convergence modes).

### Section III: Probability & Statistics (25 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Probability/probability.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Probability/probability.html)
**Tagline**: The Mathematics of Uncertainty and Inference
Classical probability and random variables; standard distributions (Gamma, Beta, Gaussian, Student's t); covariance, correlation, multivariate distributions; MLE, hypothesis testing, linear regression; entropy and information theory; convergence and limit theorems; Bayesian inference and the exponential family; Fisher information matrix and information geometry foundations; Bayesian decision theory; Markov chains, Monte Carlo, importance sampling; Gaussian processes; the **measure-theoretic deepening track** — measure-theoretic probability, product measures and limit theorems, the Radon-Nikodym theorem, conditional expectation as a Radon-Nikodym derivative — providing the foundations behind variational inference, KL-regularized policies in RLHF, score-based diffusion models, and Bayesian neural networks.

### Section IV: Discrete Mathematics & Algorithms (15 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Discrete/discrete_math.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Discrete/discrete_math.html)
**Tagline**: The Mathematics of Logic and Finite Structures
Graph theory (Eulerian / Hamiltonian, network flow, trees, planar graphs); combinatorics; Boolean logic and functional completeness; the theory of computation (finite automata, context-free languages, Turing machines, time complexity, P vs NP); the **discrete topology arc** — incidence structures and cycle / cut spaces, simplicial complexes, an introduction to homology with Betti numbers and the Euler-Poincaré formula. Three outgoing paths: cryptography and coding theory (inheriting Section I's finite-field arithmetic), computational topology and topological data analysis, and a planned category-theory / discrete-exterior-calculus track toward the Categorical Deep Learning viewpoint.

### Section V: Machine Learning (12 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Machine_learning/ml.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Machine_learning/ml.html)
**Tagline**: The Mathematics of Synthesis and Intelligence
Foundations of machine learning; regularized regression and classification; neural networks and automatic differentiation; support vector machines and kernel methods; deep neural networks (CNNs, ResNets, Self-Attention, Transformers); unsupervised learning (PCA, autoencoders, clustering); reinforcement learning (MDPs, value functions, policy gradients); natural gradient descent on statistical manifolds; variational autoencoders. Topics here serve as application viewpoints — Natural Gradient Descent already previews Riemannian geometry before manifolds are formally introduced; Variational Autoencoders already preview variational inference. Forthcoming pages on graph neural networks and equivariant networks bridge back to Section I's Lie-group series and a forthcoming smooth-manifold series in Section II.

## How to Navigate

- For **machine-readable curriculum data** (full part list, prerequisites, descriptions, anchor IDs): see [curriculum.json](https://math-cs-compass.com/curriculum.json) at the site root.
- For **interactive navigation**: the [Hexagonal Knowledge Map](https://math-cs-compass.com/map/compass.html) visualizes all topics and prerequisite paths.
- For **cross-references inside pages**: theorems and definitions carry stable IDs (`T-foo_bar`, `D-foo_bar`) consumed by the on-page hover-preview system; the registry is at `data/previews.json`.
- For **the site-wide bibliography**: see `data/references.json`.

## Technical Details

- **Platform**: Jekyll static site hosted on GitHub Pages.
- **Rendering**: MathJax for LaTeX, Mermaid for diagrams.
- **Interactive**: JavaScript / Python demos via Pyodide.
- **SEO**: Schema.org Course and LearningResource markup on each section page.

## Contact & License

- **Feedback**: Contact form available on the website.
- **License**: Educational content for free public access. Code samples follow standard open-source conventions (see GitHub).
